_note: Per-specialist case material for Copywriter. Used at workerNode (per specialty turn), not at recommendation. The specialist retrieves 2-3 cases whose `business_type_category` matches the inferred category (or `concept_first` when no business type is inferred — typical for concept-first openers), and weaves the write-vs-clarify insight into the turn without naming the source — per CLAUDE.md GR#6 ("use the case, don't cite it"). Every case here exhibits Copywriter's domain discipline: name the audience-goal-register triad that made the draft possible, show the category wallpaper the drafts replaced, and give three short alternatives the owner could paste on a sign, page, or bio and have it fit. A case that asks for more clarification without drafting is the failure mode this library exists to prevent; every `what_worked` field names the actual words that got used.

cases.challenge_pattern: solo consultant in a crowded category whose real angle — the posture their retained clients credit them with — has been hiding behind generic category copy
cases.observation: Solo AI consultant, product-management background, whose retained clients consistently said the same thing about her after a year of working together: 'she told me when AI wasn't the answer.' The homepage headline was 'AI consulting for businesses ready to unlock the power of AI' — a sentence every consultant in the category could have written. Audience was clear (technical founders and ops leaders one step past AI curiosity), goal was clear (book a discovery call), register was clear (confident, not salesy, opinionated). The angle had already been surfaced in conversation: she's the consultant clients hire to tell them when AI isn't the move. Everything needed to draft was on the table — the owner had been reaching for clarifying questions ('what's the ideal customer profile? what's the conversion goal?') that would have stalled the work.
cases.what_worked: Three versions, drafted immediately, each committing to the translator posture: (1) 'The AI consultant who will tell you when AI isn't the answer.' (2) 'Most clients come here expecting to automate. They leave with a clearer picture of what not to build.' (3) 'AI strategy for founders who want a second opinion, not a sales pitch.' The owner picked #1 and put it on the homepage that week. The audience-goal-register triad was already present; the work was committing three short lines to paper, not asking more discovery questions.
cases.what_wasted_money: Six months of copy iterations where each round asked the owner to 'clarify her ideal customer' and 'sharpen her value proposition' without ever producing a single line of actual copy. The discovery-question pattern was being mistaken for craft; it was actually a way of not drafting.
cases.one_line_lesson: When the audience, goal, and register are all on the table — and they usually are, once an earlier specialist has done their job — draft. Clarifying questions at that point are a failure mode, not a fallback.

cases.challenge_pattern: senior consultant whose homepage leads with a paragraph of credentials when the one-line version their best prospects actually forward reads completely differently
cases.observation: Senior operations consultant with two decades at Fortune 100 firms, launching independently. The homepage opened with five paragraphs about firms, titles, industries, and marquee clients. Prospects who booked discovery calls consistently described her differently than the page did — not by credentials, but by a specific move: she tells founding teams in week one when the whole strategic direction is wrong. Audience (Series B-to-D founding teams, typically mid-crisis), goal (book the strategic audit engagement), register (direct, specific, not performatively humble) were all settled. The page wasn't missing information; it was missing a sentence.
cases.what_worked: Three versions committing to the hard-week-one posture, credentials moved to the footer: (1) 'I tell founding teams what the strategic drift they're avoiding actually is — usually in the first week, usually before they wanted to hear it.' (2) 'For founding teams a quarter away from admitting the plan isn't working.' (3) 'Strategic audit engagements for leadership that wants the hard read, not the consensus one.' The owner picked #2 as the hero line and kept #1 as the about-page opener. Close rate on qualified inbound moved from 20% to 48% in the quarter.
cases.what_wasted_money: Eight months of content positioned as a peer-of-Deloitte alternative on price and credentials. Prospects who read the credentials stack arrived expecting big-firm deliverables; prospects who would have loved the hard-week-one posture bounced before they got to page two.
cases.one_line_lesson: Senior credentials are table stakes in the category. The copy is the specific move that distinguishes you from peers with identical credentials — usually the move the owner has been nervous to put on the page because it narrows the audience.

cases.challenge_pattern: small law firm where the audience is a scared person who needs warmth and clarity, but the copy is written in the register of a courtroom brief
cases.observation: Two-partner family law firm whose practice area was primarily divorces involving children. The homepage opened with a sentence about 'strategic advocacy in complex family matters' and mentioned bar memberships in the second paragraph. The actual audience was almost always a parent at the worst month of their life, typing something like 'divorce lawyer' into a phone at 10pm. The goal was to book a 30-minute consultation. The register the audience needed was warm and plainly-spoken, not procedural. The owner's instinct was that plainer language would sound 'less professional' — but the people they were writing for were not evaluating professionalism, they were evaluating whether it was safe to pick up the phone.
cases.what_worked: Three versions written at the register the audience was reading at: (1) 'If you're trying to figure out what happens next for you and your kids, we can usually talk the same week.' (2) 'Divorce lawyers who will tell you on the first call whether a long fight is even the right move.' (3) 'For parents who just need to know what the options actually are.' The firm picked #1 for the homepage and #2 for the practice-area page. Consultation bookings from mobile visitors roughly doubled in the first quarter; the owners reported most first calls started with 'your page was the first one I read that didn't make me feel worse.'
cases.what_wasted_money: Four years of bar-directory language and 'strategic advocacy' copy that was technically accurate and tonally wrong for every prospect who actually found the page. The owner had been writing to impress peers in the bar; the people paying the bills were not peers, and they were not impressed.
cases.one_line_lesson: When the audience is scared, the register is warm and clear — not procedural. The owner's instinct to sound 'professional' is usually a mismatch with what the reader needs to feel to pick up the phone.

cases.challenge_pattern: trade business with a specific craft commitment that doesn't appear anywhere on the sign, the truck, or the page — only in conversation after the quote
cases.observation: Two-person custom cabinetry shop whose lead carpenter designed joinery that didn't require face screws. Everything hidden. A specific, provable craft call. The truck vinyl and the homepage both said 'quality craftsmanship, built to last' — the same sentence every shop in the metro had on their truck. Audience (homeowners or designers commissioning kitchen and built-in work at $8-30K per project) was settled. Goal (quote requests that converted) was settled. Register (confident without being stiff, specific without being jargon-heavy) was settled. The angle — hidden joinery, not face screws — had been explicitly named in conversation. The drafts wrote themselves once the category phrases got cut.
cases.what_worked: Three versions committing to the specific craft call, each short enough for the truck: (1) 'No visible screws.' (2) 'Cabinetry built the way the best cabinetry used to be built — no face screws, ever.' (3) 'If you're going to see a screw in the finished work, we haven't finished yet.' The shop picked #1 for the truck and #3 for the homepage. Quote requests rose 30% in six months; buyers arrived referencing 'the no-screws guys.'
cases.what_wasted_money: Three years of Google Ads on 'quality cabinetry' keyword bids competing with every shop in the metro at roughly identical cost-per-click. The generic copy had no hook; the ads converted proportional to spend, not to fit. The craft commitment the owner lived by was nowhere on the page, so the wrong shoppers clicked.
cases.one_line_lesson: If the craft commitment is in the work but not in the copy, the copy is a category claim every competitor can also make. Put the commitment on the surface where the decision happens — the truck, the sign, the headline — not in a conversation after the quote.

cases.challenge_pattern: home-service business whose owner is a technical operator writing in a technical register for homeowners who are reading at a human register
cases.observation: Solo HVAC contractor in a metro market competing against two national chains. Excellent technical reputation, 8,000+ reviews averaged across fifteen years. The Google Business Profile description opened with 'HVAC installation and repair services for residential and light commercial applications with NATE-certified technicians.' Audience (homeowners, usually either in an emergency or planning a seasonal system replacement) was clear. Goal (phone call or booking request) was clear. Register — the owner was terse and technical, but his customers were not technical and were often mid-emergency, reading at 9pm from a hot kitchen. The fix wasn't the owner's tone, it was meeting the homeowner's tone.
cases.what_worked: Three versions written at the homeowner's register, not the operator's: (1) 'When your AC quits on a Tuesday night in August, we can usually get to you that evening.' (2) 'Honest HVAC. We'll tell you whether a repair or a replacement is the right move — and we'll show you the math.' (3) 'Family HVAC business. Fifteen years of same-day service calls. No pressure, no commission pitches.' The contractor picked #1 for the GBP opener and #2 for the website hero. Phone calls from mobile GBP listings rose 40% in two months.
cases.what_wasted_money: Fifteen years of technical copy that correctly described what the business did but was written at the wrong register for the moment the homeowner was reading it. The owner had been writing to peers in the trade, or to himself; the actual reader was a person who didn't care about NATE certification until after the phone call.
cases.one_line_lesson: The register follows the audience, not the owner. A terse operator writing for scared homeowners needs copy that meets the homeowner's voice, not the operator's.

cases.challenge_pattern: third-generation business adding a new offer where the copy for the new offer reads like it's for a different business than the one the customers already love
cases.observation: Third-generation neighborhood bakery launching delivery for the first time — dinner rolls, family-size cakes, and breakfast pastry bundles. The delivery-menu headline read 'Delivery services now available for online ordering.' Audience was the same audience the bakery already served for 40 years: working families in a six-block radius, many of them regulars who knew the owner's name. Goal was to get the first 100 delivery orders so the mechanic could be tested. Register was the register of the bakery itself — warm, specific, family-run, no corporate polish. The copy on the new delivery page didn't sound like the bakery. It sounded like a DoorDash template.
cases.what_worked: Three versions written in the bakery's existing voice: (1) 'Same bakery, now open past dinner. Rolls, cakes, and Sunday breakfast — to your door.' (2) 'Delivery, because you shouldn't have to leave the house for the cinnamon rolls you already know the name of.' (3) 'Saturday dinner, Sunday breakfast, Tuesday birthday cakes. Same bakery, now at your door.' The owner picked #3 for the delivery-page headline and #1 for the Instagram announcement post. First-week orders hit 40% above the forecasted mechanic-test threshold.
cases.what_wasted_money: Two weeks of landing-page copy that sounded like a DoorDash merchant template. Existing regulars, who were the test audience, didn't recognize the page as belonging to the bakery they already knew. The new offer got buried under a generic delivery register that flattened the business's voice.
cases.one_line_lesson: When a loved business adds a new offer, the new-offer copy has to sound like the business. Template register from the category flattens the voice that made the regulars loyal in the first place.

cases.challenge_pattern: restaurant whose history is the differentiator but the menu page treats it as an About-page footnote
cases.observation: Family-run Greek restaurant in Astoria, open for 35+ years under the same family. The About page told the grandfather's story with a photo — but almost no one read it; menu-scanners went to the menu and ordered. The menu page itself listed items with prices and no context. Six dishes on the menu were direct recipe translations from a specific village in the Peloponnese; customers had no way to know that from the page. Audience (neighborhood diners and weekend destination diners, half of them first-visit) was settled. Goal (menu-page click-to-reservation or walk-in decision) was settled. Register (warm, family, specific, proud without performance) was settled. The drafts were short annotations that moved the history onto the surface where the decision happened.
cases.what_worked: Three versions of a short menu opener plus item-level annotations: (1) Menu header: 'Six of these dishes come from my grandfather's village. The recipes haven't changed. Neither has our oven.' (2) Per-item annotation beside the moussaka: 'Yia-yia's 1961 recipe. Still the one we use.' (3) Per-item annotation beside the dolmathes: 'Rolled by hand. Always have been.' The owner kept all three and added similar annotations to four more items. Reservation conversions on the menu page rose visibly; more importantly, regulars started referencing specific dishes by their story in reviews.
cases.what_wasted_money: Eighteen months of social content about the grandfather and the village recipes that drove traffic to the About page but rarely converted — the path from story to purchase was broken at the menu page where the story vanished. The copy was right; it was on the wrong page.
cases.one_line_lesson: If the story is the differentiator, the story belongs on the surface where the decision happens — not behind an About-page link. A one-line history next to a menu item does more than a five-paragraph About page nobody reads.

cases.challenge_pattern: boutique studio whose philosophy is the entire differentiator but the bio leads with schedule and logistics
cases.observation: Boutique yoga studio founded by a teacher with fifteen years in a specific lineage — slow, prop-heavy, alignment-first. Materially different from the two other studios in town, both of which were power-flow. The studio's Instagram bio read 'yoga studio in [city] — all levels welcome — book a class below.' Audience (people who had outgrown power-flow, recovering from injury, or looking for something more grounded) was settled. Goal (new-student trial pack signups) was settled. Register (calm, specific, unhurried, philosophy-forward without being mystical) was settled. The angle was on every wall of the studio and in every class; it wasn't on the bio.
cases.what_worked: Three versions committing to the philosophy-forward positioning: (1) 'Slow, prop-heavy, alignment-first. Not power flow. For people who want their practice to last longer than a trend.' (2) 'Yoga for people who've moved past wanting to sweat. Iyengar-lineage teaching in [city].' (3) 'If you've outgrown fast classes — or you're recovering from one — this is for you.' The owner picked #3 for the Instagram bio and #1 for the homepage above the schedule. Trial-pack conversions on organic traffic roughly doubled.
cases.what_wasted_money: Two years of Google Ads on 'yoga near me' broad match that brought clicks but no conversions. The bio flattened a specific philosophy into a category label, so broad-match traffic arrived, found what looked like 'another yoga studio,' and bounced to the cheaper power-flow studio up the street.
cases.one_line_lesson: If the philosophy is the differentiator, the philosophy belongs in the first sentence — not above the schedule, not below the logistics. Category-label copy competes on price; philosophy-forward copy competes on fit.

cases.challenge_pattern: independent trainer whose opinionated posture is the business but the bio reads like every other independent trainer in the market
cases.observation: Solo personal trainer competing against twelve other independents and two chain-gym trainer programs. His retained clients described him the same way: he refuses to program cardio for people whose goal is strength, even when they ask, and he puts sleep ahead of workout intensity every time. The Instagram bio read 'results-driven personalized training in [city].' Audience (adults who had already tried hype-responsive trainers and washed out) was settled. Goal (DM-to-discovery-call conversion) was settled. Register (opinionated, specific, willing to lose the sale) was settled. The copy on the bio was the exact opposite of the posture the business actually had.
cases.what_worked: Three versions committing to the opinionated posture: (1) 'Strength-first programming. I won't sell you cardio you didn't come for. Sleep is the first conversation.' (2) 'Personal training for people who are tired of being hyped at. Plain programming, honest answers.' (3) 'If you've worked with a trainer who told you what you wanted to hear — try one who won't.' The trainer picked #1 for the Instagram bio, #3 for the homepage headline. Follower count dropped. Inquiries rose. Close rate on inquiries roughly doubled because the people reaching out had already self-selected for the posture.
cases.what_wasted_money: Eighteen months of client churn where hype-expecting clients signed up, got frustrated by the strength-first insistence, and left. The generic bio was attracting the wrong clients; the retention problem was really a copy problem.
cases.one_line_lesson: When the posture is the business, the bio has to name the posture — even when (especially when) the naming costs you some prospects who wouldn't have converted anyway. The exclusion is the marketing.

cases.challenge_pattern: premium product in a commodity category where the product page is written like a commodity spec sheet while the actual buyers are buying for a specific use case
cases.observation: Small-batch candle brand at $38 per candle, buying audience that was almost entirely gift-givers — the hostess dinner, the housewarming, the thank-you — not self-buyers. Product-detail pages listed ingredients, burn time, and fragrance notes: the same template shape as a $12 commodity candle. Audience (gift-givers at a specific dinner moment, not candle shoppers) was settled. Goal (category-page-to-product-page click, then add-to-cart) was settled. Register (elegant, specific, buyer-focused, not product-focused) was settled. The features copy was competing in the wrong category; the use-case copy hadn't been written.
cases.what_worked: Three versions rebuilt around the gift mechanic, features moved below the fold: (1) 'The candle you arrive with when you want to be remembered.' (2) 'For dinners where the host notices what you brought.' (3) 'A candle that says you paid attention — without saying it out loud.' The brand picked #1 as the category-page tile headline, kept #3 as the product-page hero. Landing-page conversion roughly doubled within two months.
cases.what_wasted_money: Twelve months of Meta and Google Shopping spend targeting 'premium candles' and 'soy candle gifts' at the commodity keyword layer — where a $38 candle loses on price comparison every time. The commodity-register copy on the product pages confirmed the mismatch: the audience finding them through feature-match was the wrong audience.
cases.one_line_lesson: In a commodity category, features are price-comparison bait. The copy belongs to the specific use case — the reason the buyer is hiring the product — not to the ingredient list that reads identical to the $12 alternative.

cases.challenge_pattern: concept-first founder asks for a tagline when the angle hasn't been committed yet — the copy ask is premature because Creative's job hasn't finished
cases.observation: Early-stage founder pitching a browser extension that would auto-apply coupon codes for a specific vertical — beauty-industry resellers operating on small margins who were invisible to Honey and Rakuten because they shopped at specialty suppliers, not mass retailers. The founder asked for 'a tagline for the homepage.' Audience (beauty resellers, specifically the ones currently spending hours manually stacking codes) was settled. Goal (email signup for the early-access waitlist) was settled. Register (direct, specific, industry-inside — not generic ecommerce pitch) was settled. The angle — not general-purpose coupon extension, but the one that works where Honey doesn't — had just been committed by Creative a turn earlier. Everything needed to draft was on the table.
cases.what_worked: Three versions committing to the vertical-specific angle, each short enough for a hero headline: (1) 'The coupon extension for brands Honey has never heard of.' (2) 'Auto-apply codes at the specialty suppliers Honey skips.' (3) 'For beauty resellers who spent last Tuesday stacking codes in three browser tabs.' The founder picked #1 as the headline and #3 as the first-line subheader. Waitlist signups ran at 4× the rate of the previous general-purpose positioning.
cases.what_wasted_money: Three months where the founder asked content writers for 'a great tagline' without having committed to whom the extension was for. Every draft came back generic-ecommerce-shaped because the audience wasn't named; the copy couldn't be better than the angle underneath it.
cases.one_line_lesson: When the angle isn't committed yet, the tagline can't be either — and the right move is to name that and hand back to Creative, not to draft filler. Once the angle is committed, the drafts write themselves.

cases.challenge_pattern: product-concept founder whose concept describes a feature set rather than a use case, where the one-line positioning forces the concept to commit to who it's for
cases.observation: Early-stage founder with a meditation app for parents of toddlers. The pitch described 'short guided sessions for parents' — a feature description, not a positioning. The actual use case, once Creative had forced the commitment a turn earlier, was the two-minute window that exists between a tantrum and bath time: a tired parent at 7:12pm who has ninety seconds before the next fire. Audience (parents of children under six, specifically mid-household-crisis, not wellness-seeking parents) was settled. Goal (App Store listing tap-through to install) was settled. Register (warm, plainspoken, parent-to-parent, not wellness-app serene) was settled. The drafts committed the concept to its actual moment.
cases.what_worked: Three versions of the App Store short description, each naming the specific parent and the specific ninety-second moment: (1) 'For the two minutes between the tantrum and bath time.' (2) 'Meditation for parents who don't have twenty minutes. Ninety seconds is enough.' (3) 'Built for 7:12pm on a Tuesday, when you just need the next two minutes to be quiet.' The founder picked #1 for the App Store, #3 for the homepage. Install rates from the App Store listing rose substantially once the copy told the right parent they were in the right place.
cases.what_wasted_money: Six months of App Store copy written in the wellness-app register — 'find your calm,' 'moments of mindfulness for busy parents' — that read to the exact audience as another serene-voiced app they didn't have time for. The copy was reaching the right audience and telling them they were in the wrong place.
cases.one_line_lesson: A product concept describes what the thing is; positioning copy commits to who it's for and when they'd use it. The one-line version is the shortest way to force that commitment — and the audience knows in a half-second whether the sentence is about them.
